title: Ai Agents And Tool Use
text: AI agents are systems that do more than generate text. They can plan, call tools, inspect results, and take follow-up actions based on feedback from the environment. A useful agent loop often includes goal interpretation, task decomposition, tool selection, action execution, observation, and revision. This makes agents suitable for coding assistance, research workflows, customer support orchestration, and business process automation. Tool use is important because a model's internal knowledge is limited and can become outdated. By calling a calculator, a search system, a database, or a local file reader, the agent can work with fresh information and external state. Strong agent design also requires guardrails.

title: Ai Agents And Tool Use
text: By calling a calculator, a search system, a database, or a local file reader, the agent can work with fresh information and external state. Strong agent design also requires guardrails. The system should limit risky actions, log what the agent did, and require confirmation for high-impact operations. Reliable agents are usually built around narrow responsibilities rather than unlimited autonomy.

title: Computer Vision Foundations
text: Computer vision focuses on enabling machines to interpret images and video. Classical computer vision relied on hand-engineered features, while modern systems often use convolutional neural networks or vision transformers. Applications include object detection, segmentation, medical imaging, surveillance, and autonomous driving. Vision transformers adapt the transformer idea to image patches. They model image regions as token-like inputs and learn global relationships across the visual field. This made transformer-based learning a major theme across both language and vision research.

title: Llm Safety Alignment
text: Large language model safety focuses on making model behavior helpful, honest, and less harmful. Alignment techniques try to shape outputs so the system follows intended values and constraints. Common approaches include supervised instruction tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, constitutional prompting, and policy-based refusal behavior for unsafe requests. Safety work is not only about refusal. It also includes reducing hallucinations, preventing prompt injection failures, improving factual reliability, and handling ambiguous instructions carefully. Evaluation is a major part of alignment because a system can appear safe in simple tests while failing in adversarial settings. Teams often combine automated red teaming, human review, policy checks, and dataset curation to improve safety over time.

title: Multimodal Ai Systems
text: Multimodal AI systems process more than one kind of input, such as text, images, audio, and video. A multimodal assistant might read an image, answer a question about it, and then generate a text summary. These systems are useful for medical imaging support, document understanding, visual question answering, robotics, and accessibility tools. One challenge in multimodal modeling is aligning information across modalities. The system has to connect language tokens with image regions or audio patterns in a meaningful shared representation. Training data quality matters because poor alignment data can reduce performance even when the model is large. In production systems, multimodal pipelines also need latency control, content filtering, and clear fallback behavior when one modality is missing or noisy.

title: Retrieval Augmented Generation
text: Retrieval augmented generation, often called RAG, combines document retrieval with language generation. Instead of asking a model to answer only from parametric memory, the system first retrieves relevant external knowledge. This improves factual grounding, domain adaptation, and transparency. In academic and enterprise settings, RAG is useful because the knowledge base can be updated without retraining the entire language model. The standard RAG pipeline includes document ingestion, chunking, embedding, indexing, retrieval, reranking, and answer generation. Hybrid retrieval systems combine semantic similarity with lexical signals. These systems usually perform better than single-method retrieval because they capture both exact keyword matches and broader semantic meaning. Grounded responses are important because they reduce hallucinations.

title: Retrieval Augmented Generation
text: with lexical signals. These systems usually perform better than single-method retrieval because they capture both exact keyword matches and broader semantic meaning. Grounded responses are important because they reduce hallucinations. A grounded assistant should present answers that are supported by retrieved passages. This makes the system more trustworthy for research support, policy search, and technical document exploration.

title: Transformers In Nlp
text: Transformers changed natural language processing by replacing recurrent architectures with self-attention mechanisms. Self-attention allows the model to weigh relationships between tokens regardless of distance, which improves long-range dependency modeling. Because transformer training can be parallelized efficiently, the architecture scaled well for large datasets and large model sizes. Pretraining and fine-tuning made transformers widely successful. A model can first learn general language representations on large corpora and then adapt to downstream tasks such as classification, summarization, and question answering. More recent methods include instruction tuning, retrieval augmentation, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
